coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional Fe-doped carbon dots and metal-organic frameworks nanoreactor for cascade degradation and detection of organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xuejuan
Ou, Qi
Yuan, Jiajia
Yang, Jiaojiao
Xu, Shuxia
Zhang, Xinfeng
coredata.dc:description: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are the most widely used pesticides, hence it is crucial to effectively prevent and control OPs residues for agricultural product safety and ecological development. In this work, the nanoreactor consisting of Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) and metal-organic framework (MOF-808) were developed for selective degradation as well as sensitive detection of paraoxon and parathion. MOF-808 was selected as the host and used to rapidly degrade paraoxon due to its excellent organophosphorus hydrolase activity. Fe-CDs were severed as guest molecules not only for photocatalytic degradation of parathion, but also for sensing to detect OPs. Through host–guest interaction, Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 were synthesized for cascade degradation and detection of paraoxon and parathion, respectively. Paraoxon and parathion were detoxified by catalytic hydrolysis and photocatalytic degradation by Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the nanocomposites was quenched by the internal filtering effect with 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), the degradation product of OPs. Fe-CDs/MOF-808 has a wide linear range for detection of paraoxon from 0.001 to 360 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.3 nM. Fe-CDs@MOF-808 exhibited a linear range of 0.01–100 μM for parathion, with an LOD about 3.3 nM. The reliability of the nanoreactor was verified by testing the recovery rate of OPs spiked water sample. Meanwhile, the nanoreactors were applied for degradation and detection of OPs in pakchoi. The OPs in pakchoi can be fully degraded within 5 min, and the analytical results are consistent with those by standard method (HPLC). Therefore, the proposed nanoreactor provides a feasible alternative for effective degradation and timely detection of OPs during agricultural production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paraoxon
Parathion
Carbon dots
Metal-organic frameworks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808
Fluorescence response of Fe-CDs and MOF-808 composites to OPs
The analysis of the constructed nanoreactor in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM images of MOF-808, (b) Fe-CDs/MOF-808, (c) Fe-CDs@MOF-808. (d) XPS of Fe-CDs, (e) XRD, (f) TGA, (g) BET, (h) UV-Vis and (i) Fluorescence spectra 0.5 mg/mL of MOF-808, Fe-CDs/MOF-808, Fe-CDs@MOF-808 in aqueous solution, λex = 365 nm.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of paraoxon and 4-NP in the absence and presence of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 or MOF-808. (b) The degradation rate of paraoxon by Fe-CDs/MOF-808 or MOF-808 and Fe-CDs. (c) The degradation rate of paraoxon by Fe-CDs/MOF-808 at different concentration (0–0.6 mg/mL). (d) The degradation rate of paraoxon by Fe-CDs/MOF-808 with different time. Other experimental conditions: Fe-CDs/MOF-808, MOF-808, Fe-CDs, 0.5 mg/mL, respectively; paraoxon, 4-NP, 100 μM; 9.8 mM pH 9.0 NEM buffer.

The degradation rate of parathion by Fe-CDs@MOF-808, Fe-CDs and MOF-808 after different reaction time under a 365 nm LED irradiation or without irradiation obtained by HPLC. Experimental conditions: Fe-CDs@MOF-808, MOF-808, Fe-CDs, 0.5 mg/mL, respectively; parathion, 100 μM; 9.8 mM pH 9.0 NEM buffer.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs/MOF-808, and incubated paraoxon or 4-NP. (b) Normalized absorption spectra of 4-NP and Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs/MOF-808. (c) Plot of (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentration of paraoxon. F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of the Fe-CDs/MOF-808, CDs/MOF-808, Fe-CDs system in the presence and absence of paraoxon, respectively. (d) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 incubated with various concentrations of paraoxon. (e) Plot of (F0-F)/F0 of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 at 425 nm versus the concentration of paraoxon. Inset: linear relationship at paraoxon concentration range from 0.001 to 360 μM. (f) Selectivity and anti-interference experiment using Fe-CDs/MOF-808 for detection of paraoxon. Experimental conditions: Fe-CDs/MOF-808, CDs/MOF-808, Fe-CDs, 0.5 mg/mL, respectively; paraoxon, 4-NP, and other OPs 100 μM; λex = 365 nm; 9.8 mM pH 9.0 NEM buffer.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs@MOF-808, and incubated with parathion in dark or under 365 nm LED irradiation. (b) The degradation rate of parathion catalyzed by Fe-CDs@MOF-808 in the presence of different scavengers for specific ROS. (c) GC–MS analysis of products of parathion degradation. (d) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 incubated with various concentrations of parathion under 365 nm LED irradiation. (e) Plot of (F0-F)/F0 of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 at 425 nm versus the concentration of parathion. Inset: linear relationship at parathion concentration range from 0.001 to 100 μM. F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 in the presence and absence of parathion, respectively. (f) Selectivity and anti-interference experiment using Fe-CDs@MOF-808 for detection of parathion. Experimental conditions: Fe-CDs@MOF-808, 0.5 mg/mL; parathion and other OPs, 100 μM; λex = 365 nm; 9.8 mM pH 9.0 NEM buffer.

(a) Schematic diagram of detection of OP in pakchoi. (b) Fluorescence photographs of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 adding with pakchoi in the absence and presence paraoxon (Left), reconfirming the complete degradation of paraoxon on pakchoi (Right). (c) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 adding with pakchoi in the presence paraoxon for different time, (d) Fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 adding with the pakchoi taken out of the above solution for 5 min. Experimental conditions: Fe-CDs/MOF-808, 0.5 mg/mL; paraoxon, 100 μM; 9.8 mM pH 9.0 NEM buffer.
Schematic diagram of degradation and detection of paraoxon by Fe-CD/MOF-808 (Route 1) and parathion by Fe-CD@MOF-808 (Route 2).
Comparison of the concentration of OPs in pakchoi by nanoreactor and HPLC (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Multifunctional Fe-doped carbon dots and metal-organic frameworks nanoreactor for cascade degradation and detection of organophosphorus pesticides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Lab of Geohazard Prevention & Geoenvironment Protection, College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Geohazard Prevention & Geoenvironment Protection
College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Lab of Geohazard Prevention & Geoenvironment Protection, College of Materials and Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecology and Environment, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Ecology and Environment, Chengdu University of Technology, Chengdu 610059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are the most widely used pesticides, hence it is crucial to effectively prevent and control OPs residues for agricultural product safety and ecological development. In this work, the nanoreactor consisting of Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) and metal-organic framework (MOF-808) were developed for selective degradation as well as sensitive detection of paraoxon and parathion. MOF-808 was selected as the host and used to rapidly degrade paraoxon due to its excellent organophosphorus hydrolase activity. Fe-CDs were severed as guest molecules not only for photocatalytic degradation of parathion, but also for sensing to detect OPs. Through host–guest interaction, Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 were synthesized for cascade degradation and detection of paraoxon and parathion, respectively. Paraoxon and parathion were detoxified by catalytic hydrolysis and photocatalytic degradation by Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the nanocomposites was quenched by the internal filtering effect with 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), the degradation product of OPs. Fe-CDs/MOF-808 has a wide linear range for detection of paraoxon from 0.001 to 360 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.3 nM. Fe-CDs@MOF-808 exhibited a linear range of 0.01–100 μM for parathion, with an LOD about 3.3 nM. The reliability of the nanoreactor was verified by testing the recovery rate of OPs spiked water sample. Meanwhile, the nanoreactors were applied for degradation and detection of OPs in pakchoi. The OPs in pakchoi can be fully degraded within 5 min, and the analytical results are consistent with those by standard method (HPLC). Therefore, the proposed nanoreactor provides a feasible alternative for effective degradation and timely detection of OPs during agricultural production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are generally utilized in modern agricultural production. Because of its easy preparation, low cost and high efficacy, it has been used to effectively control pests and improve crop yields [1]. Due to the high stability of OPs, their half-life in the process of hydrolytic degradation in water exceeds millions of years [2,3]. As a result, extensive and uncontrolled usage of OPs lead to a significant threat to the ecological environment and human health [4]. Meanwhile, the residues of OPs in water sources and crops can enter the human body through the food chain. OPs can bind with acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which is a critical nervous system enzyme, resulting in rapid and irreversible blocking of AChE and causing neurological disorders [5,6]. Therefore, the OPs residues in environment and food samples have attracted great concern. It is vital that a convenient and rapid technique is established to monitor OPs residues. Besides, detoxify of OPs quickly and efficiently in food or environment is also of great importance. To this end, it is desirable to develop multifunctional method endowed with both the functions of detoxification and detection of OPs.

At present, a variety of degradation and analytical methods have been established to detoxify or detect OPs [7–10]. Laboratory-based chromatography has the advantages of high sensitivity and good selectivity for OPs, but it is expensive, time-consuming, and not suitable in field detection. In addition, lots of electrochemical [11,12], colorimetric and fluorescence sensors [13–15] have been developed for OPs sensing. However, most of them are based on enzymes [7,14], such as AChE and organophosphorus hydrolase (OPH) [15,16], which also limits practical applications due to instability and high cost of enzyme. More importantly, these sensing methods failed to eliminate the toxicity of OPs while detecting so that their threats still exist.

In the past decade, metal–organic framework (MOF) as a new type of nanomaterial has been reported to have great potential in environmental governance and monitoring [17–20]. Moreover, MOFs have become a bioinspired class of materials, which can rapidly hydrolyzing these OPs and significantly diminishing their toxicity [21,22]. This is due to the strongly Lewis acidic metal ions in metal nodes of MOFs, which are excellent in activating coordinated organophosphates and decomposing the phosphate ester bond of these organophosphates [23]. Moreover, the multiple metal bridge moieties in each metal cluster are mimics of the Lewis-acidic Zn–OH–Zn active site found in OPH, an enzyme capable of efficiently hydrolyzing organophosphates [21]. In particular, zirconium-based MOFs have demonstrated outstanding catalytic performance for detoxifying organophosphate agents in basic solutions [24]. MOF-808 shows one of the fastest hydrolysis catalytic rates for methyl paraoxon in a basic solution so far, which is attributed to the large pore apertures that facilitate diffusion of the OPs into the MOF crystal and low node connectivity that increase accessibility of the OPs to the Lewis acidic Zr sites [24,25]. At same time, MOF-based luminescence sensors have been explosively developed in the detection of environmental pollutants [26,27], explosives [28], biomolecules [29], etc [30]. Generally, the luminescent properties of MOFs mainly originate from linker or metal-based emission, or sensitization and adsorbate-based emission [31]. However, lumophores bound to the MOF surface or nanopores will provide a large opportunity to create multifunctional MOFs.

Although the reported Zr-based MOFs can rapidly hydrolyze organophosphorus poisons, including organophosphorus nerve agents and paraoxon. These MOFs seem to have a low catalytic hydrolysis rate for OPs containing PS ester bond, such as parathion pesticides. Similarly, many metal nanoparticles also have OPH-like activity, such as Y2O3 [32], SiO2 [33], CeO2 [34], which can degrade OPs, but the reports are limited to paraoxon. However, as shown in Fig. S1, OPs not only contain PO ester bonds, but also OPs containing PS occupy a very large market [35]. The lack of broad-spectrum catalytic hydrolysis of OPs by Zr-MOFs, limits the application of MOFs in OPs detoxification. Fortunately, oxidants or active oxygen could attack the PS ester bond, and the P-O single bond connecting the nitrophenol or the ethyl group in parathion, leading to the formation of 4-nitrophenol or the intermediate product of paraoxon [36], which can be rapidly hydrolyzed in other ways, such as MOFs. Finally, parathion was detoxified. Therefore, an efficient and fast oxidation method and oxidation material are necessary for detoxifying parathion containing PS ester bond.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted increasing interest in recent years. Due to their optical properties, low toxicity and biocompatibility, etc. [37], CDs have shown great potential as versatile nanomaterials in many fields such as sensing [38–41], catalysis [42,43], imaging [44,45], etc. At the same time, CDs as a type of photosensitizer can activate oxygen to produce active oxygen, so it is used in photodynamic therapy [46], photocatalysis for photocatalytic energy generation [47] and environmental remediation [48].

Herein, we rationally visualized a host–guest system to construct multifunctional nanoreactors, which integrates catalytic hydrolysis, photocatalytic degradation, chemical sensing to detoxify and real-time detection of OPs, as shown in Scheme 1 . Here, MOF-808 was chosen as catalyst and host structure to rapidly hydrolyze OPs containing PO ester bonds, such as paraoxon. CDs with photocatalytic activity and sensing properties can be used not only as sensors but also as a photocatalyst to degrade OPs containing PS ester bonds, such as parathion. Furthermore, CDs by doping with Fe (III) (Fe-CDs) have higher photocatalytic activity and sensing specificity for OPs. Fe-CDs as the guest were encapsulated into the pores or loaded on the surface of MOF-808 through host–guest interaction to synthesize multifunctional nanoreactors, Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808, respectively. The results of this investigation show that MOFs-based nanocomposites can integrate hydrolysis, photocatalysis, and sensing for detoxifying and detecting paraoxon and parathion in real-time with high performance and selectivity. Finally, the practical performance of nanoreactor for OP detection in pakchoi was systematically investigated.

Paraoxon, parathion, chlorpyrifos, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, phorate, triazophos were purchased from TanMo Quality Testing Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). 1,3,5-Benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC), zirconyl chloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2·8H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), formic acid, acetone were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, ethylenediamine, N-ethylmorpholine (NEM) and methanol were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. All the reagents used in this work were of analytical grade without further purification. Ultrapure water with conductivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 was used throughout in this work.

Fluorescence spectra of the samples were collected with a F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan), Absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-5200 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Agilent 1200 system, USA.) equipped with a variable wavelength UV detector (G1314B/C) was employed to detect OPs using methanol/water mixture (V/V, 85/15) as a mobile phase based on chromatographic column (4.6 × 150 mm, 3.5 μm), and flow rate, detection wavelength, column temperature and injection volume were fixed to be 0.5 mL/min, 274 nm, 25 °C and 20.0 µL, respectively. The measured peak area was applied as an output signal to quantitatively analyze the concentration of OPs based on the plotted calibration curve. Gas chromatograph-mass spectrometer (GC–MS) (Agilent 5975C MSD, USA.) was employed to detect 4-NP based on HP-5MS chromatographic column, and flow rate, detector temperature and injection volume were fixed to be 1.0 mL/min, 290 °C,1.0 µL. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS) images of samples were obtained using a JEM-ARM200F (JEOL, Japan). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image of Fe-CDs was obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of Fe-CDs was recorded by an Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (ThermoFisher, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) were recorded by an X’Pert PRO (Malvern Panalytical) with a Cu Ka radiation; Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out with a TG209F1 (NETZSCH, Germany) under N2 with a continuous heating ramp of 10 °C/min. The apparent surface area of samples was carried out on ASAP2460 (Micromeritics, USA.) with N2 at 77 K.

Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) was synthesized via hydrothermal reaction based on previous work with some modification [49]. Firstly, citric acid (1.0507 g), FeCl3·6H2O (0.20 g) and ethylenediamine (335 µL) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL). Then the solution was transferred to 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 250 °C for 5 h. The reactor was gradually cooled down to room temperature. The obtained brown-black product was further purified by gel permeation chromatography (water as eluent and silica 400 mesh as stationary phase). The Fe-CDs powders with uniform particles were obtained after drying. Carbon dots (CDs) were also synthesized by the same method except that FeCl3·6H2O was not added.

MOF-808 samples were prepared via the reported method [50]. In brief, H3BTC (0.1175 g, 0.56 mmol) and ZrOCl2·8H2O (0.54 g, 1.67 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL mixture of DMF and formic acid (V/V, 1/1). Then the above solution was transferred to 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 48 h. The obtained white product was washed thoroughly with DMF and acetone for several times and dried under vacuum at 25 °C. MOF-808 was activated under vacuum at 120 °C for 12 h.

Fe-CD/MOF-808 were synthesized by Fe-CDs and precursors of MOF-808. In brief, 2 mL 0.3 g/mL Fe-CDs solution, H3BTC (0.1175 g, 0.56 mmol) and ZrOCl2·8H2O (0.54 g, 1.67 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL mixture of DMF and formic acid (V/V, 1/1). Then the following steps are carried out with the synthesis of MOF-808.

Fe-CDs@MOF-808 were synthesized by Fe-CDs and MOF-808. Firstly, 2 mL Fe-CDs solution without purification via chromatography and 150 mg MOF-808 were stirred at room temperature for 12 h to obtain Fe-CDs@MOF-808. The brown precipitates were washed thoroughly several times with DMF, ultrapure water, and acetone. Then Fe-CDs@MOF-808 were dried and activated under vacuum at 120 °C for 12 h.

In a typical process, different amounts of freshly prepared paraoxon (0–360 μM), 0.5 mg/mL MOF-808 or Fe-CDs/MOF-808 were introduced into 9.8 mM, pH 9.0 NEM buffer solution. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the above solutions were recorded after incubating at room temperature for 5 min. In order to study the selectivity of the strategy for paraoxon, other OPs and mixture were used to replace paraoxon in the same experiments, including parathion, chlorpyrifos, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, phorate, triazophos. The residual paraoxon in the filtrate obtained by centrifugation was determined by HPLC after the reaction of paraoxon with MOF-808 or Fe-CDs/MOF-808. And the degradation rate of paraoxon was calculated by the equation, i.e., (1-C/C0) × 100%, where C0 and C were the concentration of before and after the degradation of paraoxon, respectively.

In a typical process, different amounts of freshly prepared parathion (0–100 μM), 0.5 mg/mL MOF-808 or Fe-CDs@MOF-808 were introduced into 9.8 mM, pH 9.0 NEM buffer solution. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the reaction solutions were measured under 365 nm LED irradiation (3 V, 3 W) for 10 min. The residual parathion in the filtrate obtained by centrifugation was determined by HPLC after the reaction of parathion with MOF-808 or Fe-CDs@MOF-808. In order to study the selectivity of the strategy for parathion, the same experiments were performed using other OPs and mixture, including paraoxon, chlorpyrifos, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, phorate, triazophos to replace parathion. And the degradation rate of parathion was calculated by the equation, i.e., (1 − C/C0) × 100%, where C0 and C were the concentration of before and after the degradation of parathion, respectively.

The pakchoi and real water samples were obtained at a local supermarket, lake, river and farmland, respectively. Firstly, a piece of pakchoi (∼0.1 g) was immersed in solution 1000 μM paraoxon for 1 min, then the piece of pakchoi contaminated by OPs was transferred into 2.0 mL, 0.5 mg/mL Fe-CDs/MOF-808 solution. The fluorescence spectra and images of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 was recorded after different reaction time. After there is no change in the fluorescence signal of the solution, the piece of pakchoi was taken and placed it in a new suspension of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 to observe the fluorescence emission and record it.

Secondly, in order to evaluate the accuracy of our detection method, OPs were extracted from the contaminated pakchoi and detected by our sensing method and HPLC, respectively. Pieces of pakchoi (0.1 g) was immersed in 1000 μM and 100 μM paraoxon and parathion for 1 min, respectively. Then the samples were extracted by 0.5 mL mixture of methanol and water (V/V, 7/3) containing 0.02 g NaCl, and the extracts were directly analyzed by the above experimental procedure and HPLC.

Thirdly, real water samples were firstly filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then added with different concentrations (6, 0.5, 0.05 μM) of paraoxon and parathion to obtain the contaminated water samples. The as-prepared positive samples were then analyzed according the above experimental procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal polymerization of citric acid, iron (III) chloride hexahydrate and ethylenediamine. For Fe-CDs/MOF-808, the Fe-CDs were encapsulated into nanopores of MOFs, which were obtained through hydrothermal synthesis by Fe-CDs and MOF-808. Immobilization of Fe-CDs in the surface of MOFs-808 was achieved by the reaction between Fe-CDs and MOF-808 at room temperature, denoted as Fe-CDs@MOF-808. Detailed physical characterizations about the above Fe-CDs-loaded MOF-808 are given in Fig. 1 . The morphology of MOF-808 was studied by SEM, and it possesses an octahedral morphology with a particle size of 400–500 nm (Fig. 1a). Moreover, Fe-CDs were located on MOF surface or inside the MOF nanopores, the morphology and size of MOF-808 have not changed significantly (Fig. 1b and c), which is due to the relatively small size of Fe-CDs (Fig. S2). XPS analysis was further used to verify the composition of Fe-CDs, confirming also the presence of nitrogen, oxygen and iron species in the CDs (Fig. 1d). The EDS data revealed that the MOF-808 composites contain Fe, indicating that Fe-CDs successfully loaded with MOF-808 (Table S1). The obtained samples showed XRD reflexes matching simulated patterns from single crystals, confirming crystallinity (Fig. 1e). The XRD peaks of both Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 samples are not obviously altered. These results indicate that the presence of Fe-CDs does not affect the morphology and crystal structure of MOF. TGA experiments under N2 revealed a decomposition temperature onset shift from Fe-CDs@MOF-808 compared to the pristine MOF (Fig. 1f, Fig. S3). That is attributed to Fe-CDs being more thermally stable than the pristine MOF, providing further evidence that Fe-CDs are successfully immobilized on MOFs [51]. For Fe-CDs/MOF-808, the decomposition temperature and trend are basically the same as that the pristine MOF, which means that the Fe-CDs in the nanopores of MOF-808 didn’t affect the collapse of MOF.

N2 adsorption measurement of MOF-808 loaded with Fe-CDs displayed significant uptake differences. As shown in Fig. 1g, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface is 702.37 m2 g−1 for Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and 1016.03 m2 g−1 for Fe-CDs@MOF-808, which is lower than that of MOF-808 (surface area of 1169.63 m2 g−1). Table S2 show the BET surface, pore size distributions and the pore volume data. The pore size and volume of MOF-808 composites are smaller than MOF-808, which demonstrates a large amount of Fe-CDs occupy the surface of MOF-808. The lowering of the surface area and pore size further indicated the successful loading of the Fe-CDs in MOF-808. For Fe-CDs/MOF-808, these data have reduced the most, which is reasonable as the Fe-CDs enters the nanopores of MOF-808 due to the internal surface of cavities being the main contributor to BET surface area. In contrast, Fe-CDs in Fe-CDs@MOF-808 remains on the outer surface of MOF-808 without fully blocking nanopores of MOF-808.

Optical properties of nanoreactors are characterized by UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectra. Fe-CDs@MOF-808 exhibited absorption peak similar to that of Fe-CDs in its UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 1h and Fig. S4 a), but this absorption peak completely disappeared in Fe-CDs/MOF-808, just like MOF-808, which is due to Fe-CDs embedded in nanopores of MOF-808. The fluorescence spectra of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 showed an emission peak at ∼425 nm similar to that of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1i and Fig. S4 b). These results explain that Fe-CDs in Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 composites are located on the nanopores and surface of MOF-808, respectively.

In order to verify that the fabricated nanoreactors can quickly degrade OPs, we firstly used MOF-808 with organophosphorus hydrolase activity to catalyze the hydrolysis of 100 μM paraoxon. As shown in the Fig. 2 a, when MOF-808 is present in the system, the absorption peak of paraoxon at 275 nm disappears after 5 min. In contrast, the peak of the product 4-NP at 400 nm appears, and it is consistent with the absorption spectrum of 100 μM 4-NP, indicating that paraoxon is completely hydrolyzed by MOF-808. The HPLC results also show that MOF-808 completely catalyzes the hydrolysis of paraoxon under these conditions (Fig. 2b and Fig. S5). For Fe-CDs, the HPLC results show that it cannot catalyze the degradation of paraoxon (Fig. 2b and Fig. S6). Moreover, Fe-CDs/MOF-808 has the same catalytic hydrolysis rate for paraoxon as MOF-808 (Fig. 2a and b, Fig. S5), which may be due to that Fe-CDs encapsulated into the nanopores will not affect the metal catalytic sites on the MOF surface. We conducted optimization screening of the concentration of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 during the degradation of paraoxon. As shown in Fig. 2c, the results turned out that the degradation rate of paraoxon obviously increased with the increase of catalyst concentration, until the concentration of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 increased to 0.4 mg/mL or higher, paraoxon was basically completely degraded. In order to ensure complete detoxification of OPs, 0.5 mg/mL of nanoreactor was selected as the optimal catalyst dosage. We also tested the degradation rate of paraoxon at different times in the presence of Fe-CDs/MOF-808. As shown in Fig. 2d, 100 μM paraoxon was reacted with Fe-CDs/MOF-808 for 3 min and the degradation rate of paraoxon reached 83.8%, and it was completely degraded after 5 min. However, Fe-CDs@MOF-808 cannot fully catalyze the hydrolytic degradation of paraoxon under this condition (Fig. S7). This is because the Fe-CDs on the surface of the MOF occupies part of the catalytic hydrolysis sites, which reduces the hydrolysis rate of paraoxon.

Parathion, a stubborn and toxic organophosphorus pesticide, has a very similar chemical structure to paraoxon, only the difference between PO and PS. However, as shown in Fig. S8, experimental results showed that parathion was only slightly degraded by MOF-808 or Fe-CDs/MOF-808 after 30 min. Fe-CDs with high photosensitivity has been used for degradation of pollutants [52]. Therefore, we considered whether Fe-CDs with photocatalytic activity can be used to oxidize and degrade parathion through active oxygen generated from photosensitization. More importantly, host–guest interactions of Fe-CDs and MOF-808 will provide a higher photocatalytic degradation efficiency for parathion. Experimental research of photocatalytic degradation of parathion under 365 nm LED irradiation was carried out to assess the photocatalytic activity of Fe-CDs@MOF-808, because active sites of Fe-CDs could probably be covered by MOF-808 in the Fe-CDs/MOF-808 material (Fig. S9). Hence, Fe-CDs@MOF-808 was used to degrade parathion by photocatalytic oxidation. MOF-808 has no photocatalytic activity (Fig. S10), and Fe-CDs can degrade only part of parathion under 365 nm LED irradiation for 15 min (Fig. S11). Interestingly, the parathion peak on HPLC has been significantly reduced after reacting with Fe-CDs@MOF-808 under 365 nm LED irradiation for 5 min, and disappeared completely at 10 min, indicating that the parathion has been completely degraded (Fig. S12). The degradation rate of parathion under different irradiation time is displayed in Fig. 3 d. For pure MOF-808, it can be found that the degradation rate of parathion only increased from 26.2% to 51.9% within 30 min. This is because part of the parathion is hydrolyzed by MOF-808 with organophosphorus hydrolase activity. For Fe-CDs, the parathion degradation rates increased from 7.5% to 71.9% within 30 min. Compared to pristine Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs@MOF-808 can significantly and quickly degrade parathion, the degradation rate can reach 98.1% for 10 min and completely degraded for 30 min. However, the degradation rate of parathion did not change in the presence of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 without lamp irradiation (Fig. 3). The degradation rate about 30% without irradiation time is due to the enzyme-like activity of MOF-808 that can hydrolyze organophosphates in the dark. Therefore, photocatalytic degradation is the main route to detoxify parathion. During the whole degradation process of parathion, 30 min was selected as the photoreaction time of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 and parathion. It was concluded that the introduction of MOF-based host–guest matrix is an effective and valuable approach to improve the photocatalytic activity of Fe-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Fe-CDs and MOF-808 composites to OPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-CDs and MOF-808 composites can not only quickly catalyze OPs degradation, but also can be used as a sensing platform for selective detection of OPs. It can serve as nanoreactor that integrates degradation and detection together. Firstly, as shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence emission of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 was obviously quenched after adding 100 μM paraoxon for 10 min, which is consistent with the quenching of 100 μM 4-NP added to the system. It has been shown that the fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 is due to the production of 4-NP after the hydrolysis of paraoxon. It also shows that degradation and detection can occur in cascade in this system. Because the absorption curve of 4-NP overlaps with the fluorescence emission of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 (as shown in Fig. 4b), the internal filtering effect is the main cause of fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs/MOF-808.

Since Fe-CDs is the main fluorescence emission signal of the nanoreactor, we investigated the changes of the fluorescence emission of Fe-CDs, CD/MOF-808, and Fe-CDs/MOF-808 after adding different concentrations of paraoxon. As shown in Fig. 4c, paraoxon didn’t affect the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs, because Fe-CDs cannot hydrolyze paraoxon. For CDs/MOF-808 or Fe-CDs/MOF-808, the fluorescence emission changes significantly after adding paraoxon, especially the composite doped with Fe in CDs. This may be due to the interaction between Fe (III) and 4-NP, making Fe-CD/MOF-808 more responsive to substrates [53,54].

Further, the sensitivity of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 to paraoxon were also investigated. As the Fig. 4d shows, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 clearly decreased with the addition of paraoxon in the range of 0.001–360 μM. The (F0-F)/F0 of Fe-CDs/MOF-808 at 425 nm was high in relation to paraoxon dosage (CPX) (0.001–360 μM) with high correlation (R2) at 0.9955 (Fig. 4e). The formula was tested to be (F0-F)/F0 = 0.1447 + 0.3069 lg(CPX + 0.5) with the limit of detection (LOD) at 0.3 nM, which is much lower than 0.1 μg/mL (36 μM legal maximum). In comparison with methods previously reported (Table S3), our assay displayed wider detection range and faster response speed. Overall, the sensitivity and LOD of our strategy for paraoxon are enough for practical applications. Moreover, the proposed technology does not involve any biological elements such natural enzymes, antibodies or aptamers, etc.

In order to study the specificity of the assay for detecting paraoxon, other OPs such as parathion, chlorpyrifos, dichlorvos, malathion, omethoate, phorate, and trizophos were also tested by the constructed Fe-CDs/MOF-808 nanoreactor. Fig. 4f showed that only paraoxon has an obvious fluorescence signal change, demonstrating the high selectivity of the sensing system. Also, we investigate the anti-interference of using Fe-CDs/MOF-808 for detection of paraoxon by mixing other OPs with paraoxon. The result in Fig. 4f reveals that the presence of other OPs didn’t affect the fluorescence emission of Fe-CDs/MOF-808. Such features make the method fast, intuitive, low-cost and specificity for practical applications in the detection of paraoxon.

Secondly, the Fe-CDs@MOF-808 has been used for real-time detection of parathion. As shown in Fig. 5 a, Fe-CDs@MOF-808 can quickly degrade and detect parathion, according to its fluorescence emission was quenched after adding parathion under irradiation. But the fluorescence emission of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 didn’t decreased by parathion without irradiation. This is mainly because Fe-CDs@MOF-808 generates singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion (O2 –), hydroxyl radical (OH), and electron hole (h+) under 365 nm LED irradiation (Fig. 5b), and photocatalytic degradation of parathion and produces 4-NP (Fig. 5c, Figs. S13–S15).

We also explore the sensitivity for detection parathion. The emission intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 at 425 nm gradually decreased with the raising of parathion dosage. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 and parathion concentration was displayed in Fig. 5d. There was a good relationship between (F0-F)/F0 of Fe-CDs@MOF-808 and parathion dosage (CPT) (R2 = 0.9938) in the range of 0.01–100 μM (Fig. 5e). The calibration curve was expressed as the equation (F0-F)/F0 = 0.1256 + 0.0597 lg(CPT), and the LOD was 3.3 nM, which was lower than most previously reported methods in Table S4. Other OPs like chlorpyrifos, omethoate, malathion, dichlorvos, trizaophos etc., were used to reveal the specificity and anti-interference ability of this method. The result in Fig. 5f demonstrated that the target analyte parathion leads to a much higher (F0-F)/F0 value comparing with other OPs. The interference from other OPs was further investigated by mixing parathion with them. Fig. 5f exhibited that some OPs possessed positive interferences (about 20% for phorate and chlorphyrifos).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of the constructed nanoreactor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practicability of the nanoreactor for OPs degradation and sensing, the assay was evaluated by spraying OPs onto pakchoi and spiking OPs into water samples, respectively.

Additionally, water samples from lake, river and farmland water were analyzed. However, the values of OPs were not detectable by these nanoreactor. Then, OPs standards of 0.05, 0.5, and 6 μM were spiked to these samples, respectively. The results were shown in Table S5. Its recovery was satisfactory, ranging from 90 % to 114 %, which suggested that this new assay method may have great potential in practical applications. These results indicate that nanoreactor has the potential for rapid, low-cost, and in-situ degradation and visual detection of organophosphorus compound pollution.

In conclusion, MOF-based nanoreactors were successfully constructed that integrate catalytic hydrolysis activity, photocatalytic activity, and sensing performance for OPs. Given the different catalytically active sites to be exposed, we synthesized Fe-CDs/MOF-808 with high catalytic hydrolysis activity for paraoxon and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 with photocatalytic activity for parathion, respectively. Meanwhile, due to Fe-CDs doping, these MOF-808-based complexes can selectively detect paraoxon and parathion during different degradation processes. Therefore, Fe-CDs/MOF-808 and Fe-CDs@MOF-808 can achieve degradation and simultaneous analysis of paraoxon and parathion, respectively. The proposed nanoreactors demonstrate wide detection linear ranges, low LOD, and high recovery rates. Meanwhile, pakchoi sample was sprayed with OPs to simulate contaminated vegetables, and the detoxication of the pesticides could be quickly observed by simply soaking the pakchoi in a solution containing the nanoreactors. The reliability of the nanoreactor was further confirmed by measuring the recovery of OPs added in different water samples. This work could provide a new idea for exploring and utilizing of nanocomposites integrating detoxification with detection to achieve food safety and environmental governance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Sichuan Science and Technology Project (No. 2022NSFSC1087) and the Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials (No. sklssm2023035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142480.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
